sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Testicular torsion is twisting of the spermatic cord that cuts off the blood supply to the testis. It is a **time-critical urological emergency**.

### Time to Salvage Rates
| Time to Surgical Detorsion | Testicular Salvage |
|---|---|
| <6 hours | ~100% |
| 6–12 hours | ~50% |
| 12–24 hours | ~10% |
| >24 hours | Near 0% |

**Do not delay evacuation.** The window is short and closed consultations waste irreplaceable time.

### Who Gets Testicular Torsion
- Peak incidence: **12–25 years** — the exact demographic of much of the FIFO workforce
- Can occur at any age including neonates and older men
- Predisposing anatomy: **"bell-clapper" deformity** — high horizontal testicular lie; often bilateral
- Precipitants: physical activity, minor trauma, cold exposure, or **no precipitant at all** (may occur during sleep)

### Missed Torsion
Testicular torsion is one of the most commonly missed diagnoses in young men presenting with scrotal pain — often misdiagnosed as epididymo-orchitis and treated with antibiotics, resulting in loss of the testis.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
### Classic Presentation
- **Sudden onset, severe, unilateral testicular/scrotal pain**
- Pain may radiate to lower abdomen, groin, or flank
- **Nausea and vomiting** — very common (from vagal response to pain)
- Ipsilateral testis is high-riding (elevated and lies transversely)
- **Absent cremasteric reflex** ipsilaterally — the most clinically reliable sign

### The Cremasteric Reflex
- Stroke the inner thigh → normal response = ipsilateral testis rises
- **Absent cremasteric reflex = testicular torsion until proven otherwise**
- Present reflex does not completely exclude torsion (30% false negative in confirmed torsion)

### Signs Developing Over Time
- Scrotal oedema and erythema (may develop within hours)
- Tender, firm testis
- Horizontal lie of testis
- Low-grade fever (late, from ischaemic inflammation)

### Distinguishing from Epididymo-Orchitis
| Feature | Testicular Torsion | Epididymo-Orchitis |
|---|---|---|
| Onset | Sudden | Gradual over hours-days |
| Age | Typically young | Any age; older more common |
| Fever | Absent or late | Common |
| Urinary symptoms | Absent | Often present (dysuria, discharge) |
| Cremasteric reflex | Absent | Usually present |
| Prehn's sign | Negative (elevation worsens) | Positive (elevation relieves) |
| Urinalysis | Normal | May show leucocytes |

**If uncertain: treat as torsion and evacuate.**

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
### Clinical Diagnosis — Do Not Wait for Investigations
- Testicular torsion is a **clinical diagnosis** in the field
- Do NOT wait for ultrasound, urinalysis, or other investigations
- Doppler ultrasound is the investigation of choice at hospital — but must never delay evacuation
- A missed torsion results in loss of the testis and often permanent fertility impact

### Time of Pain Onset Must Be Documented
- The 6-hour window starts from **onset of pain, not from time of presentation**
- If patient has had pain for 4 hours before presenting, there are only 2 hours of remaining viable window
- Communicate onset time clearly to RFDS

### Antibiotics Are NOT the Answer
- Do not treat presumed "epididymo-orchitis" with antibiotics without excluding torsion
- If uncertain: evacuate for urgent surgical assessment

### Manual Detorsion
- Can be attempted if evacuation is significantly delayed
- Technique: rotate the testis **lateral to medial** ("opening a book") — left testis counterclockwise, right testis clockwise when viewed from below
- Successful detorsion = rapid, dramatic pain relief
- **Even if successful, urgent surgical fixation (orchidopexy) is still required** — torsion can recur and contralateral fixation is also performed

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
### Immediate Actions
1. **Call RFDS immediately** — time-critical surgical emergency
2. **Document time of pain onset** — communicate to RFDS
3. **Nil by mouth** — surgical candidate
4. **IV access**
5. **Analgesia** — aggressive

### Analgesia
- Morphine 2.5–5 mg IV titrated, or fentanyl 25–50 mcg IV
- Ondansetron 4–8 mg IV for nausea/vomiting
- Scrotal elevation (on a towel) reduces discomfort from oedema

### Attempted Manual Detorsion (If Evacuation Significantly Delayed)
1. Administer adequate IV analgesia first
2. Stand at patient's feet; rotate testis lateral to medial
3. Left testis: rotate counterclockwise (viewed from below)
4. Right testis: rotate clockwise (viewed from below)
5. Successful if pain dramatically decreases
6. May require 1.5 turns (540 degrees)
7. **Evacuate regardless of outcome** — orchidopexy still required

### Communication to RFDS
- Time of pain onset (exact)
- Examination findings: cremasteric reflex absent/present, testis position, oedema
- Any attempted manual detorsion and outcome

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
### Consideration of Other Acute Scrotal Conditions
If torsion cannot be confirmed or excluded and evacuation is significantly delayed:

**Torsion of the appendix testis:**
- Less severe pain, gradual onset, "blue dot sign" (small blue-black nodule visible through scrotal skin)
- Managed conservatively (analgesia, anti-inflammatories)
- Still requires medical review

**Incarcerated inguinal hernia:**
- Scrotal or inguinal swelling, unable to reduce
- Bowel obstruction symptoms (nausea, vomiting, inability to pass flatus)
- Surgical emergency

**Fournier's gangrene:**
- Necrotising fasciitis of the perineum
- Systemic sepsis, rapidly spreading erythema and crepitus of scrotum/perineum
- Immediate IV antibiotics (meropenem or cefazolin + metronidazole) and urgent surgery

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
### After Surgical Treatment
- Orchidopexy (bilateral testicular fixation) is performed surgically
- Contralateral testis is fixed at the same time
- Fertility may be affected even after successful detorsion (ischaemic damage)
- Return to physical work: typically 2–4 weeks post-surgery
